scenario: You are writing a tool description for a send_email tool that can both draft and send emails. Claude keeps using it to send emails when users only want to draft them.
question: What is missing from this tool description?
options: The tool needs a more creative name
The tool description must include WHAT it does, WHEN to use it, and explicitly WHEN NOT to use it (e.g., 'Do not use this tool to send an email if the user only asked to draft or preview it')
The tool needs more parameters so Claude can infer intent better
The tool_choice should be set to 'auto' to give Claude full discretion
explanation: A complete tool description must include: (1) WHAT the tool does mechanically, (2) WHEN to use it (triggering conditions), and (3) WHEN NOT to use it (exclusion conditions). The third element is most commonly omitted and causes exactly the described problem — Claude uses a capable tool when it shouldn't. Adding an explicit 'Do not call this tool when the user only wants to draft or preview' rule eliminates the ambiguity. More parameters (C) and tool_choice auto (D) won't help if the description doesn't establish the boundary.

scenario: You have an agent that must always call at least one tool before responding. You want to prevent Claude from responding with plain text without making a tool call.
question: Which tool_choice setting enforces this?
options: tool_choice: { type: 'auto' } — Claude decides whether to use a tool
tool_choice: { type: 'any' } — Claude must call one of the available tools
tool_choice: { type: 'tool', name: 'specific_tool' } — forces a specific tool call
Remove tool_choice from the request — Claude will always use tools if available
explanation: tool_choice: { type: 'any' } forces Claude to call at least one tool from the available set, but gives it freedom to choose which one. tool_choice: auto (A) is the default — Claude may respond with plain text if it judges no tool is needed. Forcing a specific tool (C) would work only if there's exactly one appropriate tool. Omitting tool_choice (D) defaults to auto. The three modes: auto (Claude decides), any (must call a tool, Claude picks), specific tool (must call that exact tool).

scenario: You have built a customer service agent with 25 tools covering account management, billing, shipping, returns, and technical support. You notice Claude is frequently calling the wrong tool.
question: What architectural change will most improve tool selection accuracy?
options: Improve the tool descriptions with more detailed parameter explanations
Increase the system prompt size to explain each tool more thoroughly
Split the 25 tools across multiple specialized MCP servers (5-8 tools each), and use a coordinator that delegates to the appropriate server
Switch to a higher-temperature setting to encourage Claude to explore more tool options
explanation: Tool selection accuracy degrades significantly beyond ~18 tools in context. With 25 tools, you're well past the degradation cliff. The architectural fix is MCP server segmentation: create specialized servers (billing-server, shipping-server, returns-server, etc.) each with 5-8 focused tools. A coordinator routes to the right server, which then has a much smaller and focused tool set. Better descriptions (A, B) help marginally but don't address the fundamental cognitive load issue. Temperature (D) doesn't improve selection accuracy.

scenario: A tool call to your external API fails with a 503 Service Unavailable error. Your tool needs to return a result to Claude.
question: What is the correct tool_result structure for this failure?
options: Return an empty tool_result and let Claude infer something went wrong
Throw an exception in the tool handler to abort the conversation
Return a tool_result with is_error: true and a structured content block containing the error type, message, and retryable: true
Return a tool_result with the error as plain text: '503 error occurred'
explanation: The correct pattern is a structured tool_result with is_error: true and a content block that includes: error type (503/service_unavailable), human-readable message, and a retryable flag. The retryable: true field is critical — it tells Claude (and the coordinator) that this is a transient failure that can be retried, not a permanent one. An empty result (A) causes Claude to proceed with bad assumptions. Exceptions (B) crash the loop. Plain text errors (D) require Claude to parse the error, which is fragile.

scenario: You are designing an MCP server for a file management system. Some operations need to be performed (write_file, delete_file) while others are informational (list_files, get_file_metadata).
question: How should you differentiate these in MCP server design?
options: Implement all of them as tools — tools are the universal MCP primitive
Implement write_file and delete_file as tools (actions that change state), and list_files / get_file_metadata as resources (read-only data access)
Implement all as resources with a read_only: true flag on informational ones
Use prompts for list_files and tools for write_file and delete_file
explanation: MCP has three primitives with distinct purposes: Tools = actions that execute code and change state (write, delete, send); Resources = read-only data access (metadata, catalogs, listings); Prompts = reusable prompt templates. Using resources for reads and tools for writes is the correct semantic split. It also enables clients to understand that resource calls are safe/read-only while tool calls may have side effects. Implementing everything as tools (A) works but loses semantic clarity and safety guarantees.

scenario: You need Claude to query two independent data sources in one turn: one to get customer data and another to get order history. These queries have no dependency on each other.
question: What is the most efficient implementation?
options: Sequential: query customer data first, then query order history in the next turn
Submit both tool calls in the same assistant turn as parallel tool calls, then collect both results in one user turn
Combine both queries into a single tool that accepts both IDs
Use streaming to interleave the two queries
explanation: Parallel tool calls — submitting multiple tool_use blocks in a single assistant turn — is exactly designed for independent operations. Claude sends both requests simultaneously, you execute them in parallel, and return both tool_results in a single user turn. This halves the round-trip latency vs. sequential. A combined tool (C) is a valid approach but tightly couples two concerns and is less flexible. Streaming (D) reduces time-to-first-token but doesn't parallelize execution. Sequential (A) wastes time when there's no dependency.

scenario: You are defining the JSON Schema for a tool parameter 'status' that can be: 'pending', 'active', 'suspended', or occasionally a custom string value the system doesn't yet know about.
question: How should you define this parameter in the schema?
options: type: 'string' with no constraints — accept anything
enum: ['pending', 'active', 'suspended'] — strict enum, reject unknowns
enum: ['pending', 'active', 'suspended', 'other'] with an additional 'custom_status' string field for when 'other' is selected
type: 'integer' mapping statuses to numbers for efficiency
explanation: The 'other' escape pattern is the correct schema design for enums that may need to accommodate unknown values. Define the known values as enum entries, add 'other' as an escape hatch, and provide a companion string field for the custom value. This preserves type safety for known values while gracefully handling the long tail. Unconstrained string (A) loses all type safety. Strict enum without 'other' (B) breaks for unexpected values — and unknown values will occur. Integer mappings (D) are opaque and maintenance-heavy.

scenario: You are building an MCP server with a tools catalog. When Claude needs to browse available operations before deciding which one to call, what MCP primitive is best suited for exposing the catalog?
question: Which MCP primitive should expose the operations catalog?
options: A tool called list_available_operations
A resource — resources are designed for metadata and catalog reads before actions are taken
A prompt template that lists all operations
Embed the catalog in every tool description
explanation: MCP Resources are specifically designed for read-only data that informs decision-making — exactly what a catalog is. Claude can read the resource to understand what's available before deciding which tool to call. This is the 'resources for metadata reads before actions' pattern. A tool (A) would work but is semantically wrong — tools are for actions with side effects. Prompt templates (C) are for reusable prompt patterns, not dynamic catalogs. Embedding in every description (D) bloats the tool context unnecessarily.

scenario: You need to ensure that whenever Claude calls a tool, the input always includes required fields and never includes unexpected fields.
question: What JSON Schema configuration enforces this?
options: Mark all fields as optional and validate server-side
Set strict: true in the tool schema and define all required fields in the 'required' array
Add descriptions to each field explaining they are required
Validate inputs in a PreToolUse hook and return an error if invalid
explanation: strict: true in the tool schema does two things: (1) enforces that all 'required' fields are present, and (2) disallows additional properties not defined in the schema. This is the API-level enforcement mechanism. Descriptions (C) are guidance for Claude, not constraints. PreToolUse hooks (D) can catch this but it's the wrong layer — the schema should prevent invalid calls before they happen. Making fields optional (A) defeats the purpose. Always define your required fields in the 'required' array AND set strict: true.

scenario: An MCP server has grown to 22 tools over time. You notice Claude is making incorrect tool selections in 30% of cases. You cannot reduce the number of features.
question: What is the correct architectural fix?
options: Rewrite all tool descriptions to be shorter and more concise
Increase the system prompt token budget to explain each tool more thoroughly
Split the 22 tools into 3-4 focused MCP servers (5-8 tools each), and route to the appropriate server based on the request domain
Use tool_choice: auto and trust Claude to improve with a better system prompt
explanation: This is a direct manifestation of the 18-tool degradation cliff. When tool count exceeds ~18, selection accuracy drops sharply regardless of description quality. The fix is architectural: split into focused MCP servers. Each server has 5-8 tools in a specific domain, so Claude's effective tool count per request is well below the threshold. Conciseness (A) and larger system prompts (B) marginally help but don't address the fundamental issue. tool_choice: auto (D) with a better prompt cannot overcome the underlying cognitive load problem.

scenario: Your agent has 25 tools available. During testing, you notice Claude frequently selects the wrong tool or fails to use tools when it should, especially for similar-sounding tools.
question: What is the primary cause and recommended fix according to Anthropic's best practices?
options: Claude has a bug with large tool sets — file a support ticket and wait for a fix
Beyond ~18 tools, Claude's tool selection accuracy degrades significantly; reduce the active tool set by grouping related tools or dynamically loading only relevant tools per turn
Add 'IMPORTANT: always use tools when available' to the system prompt to force usage
Increase max_tokens to give Claude more space to reason about tool selection
explanation: Anthropic documents that tool selection degrades beyond ~18 tools. The fix is architectural: reduce the active set through grouping, dynamic loading, or hierarchical tool organization (coordinator-subagent). It's not a bug — it's an attention-dilution effect. Forcing tool use creates false positives. max_tokens controls output length, not reasoning about tool selection.

scenario: You want Claude to ALWAYS call your 'log_interaction' tool after every response, regardless of conversation content. Claude should not decide whether to call it.
question: Which tool_choice configuration enforces mandatory tool invocation of a specific tool?
options: tool_choice: {type: 'auto'} with a system prompt saying 'always use log_interaction'
tool_choice: {type: 'tool', name: 'log_interaction'} which forces Claude to call that specific tool
tool_choice: {type: 'any'} which forces Claude to call at least one tool but not necessarily log_interaction
tool_choice: {type: 'none'} to disable all other tools so only log_interaction remains
explanation: tool_choice with type 'tool' and a specific name forces Claude to call exactly that tool. 'auto' lets Claude decide (unreliable for mandatory calls even with prompting). 'any' forces SOME tool but not a specific one — Claude picks whichever seems relevant. 'none' disables all tool use entirely, including the desired tool.

scenario: You're designing a tool that queries a flaky external API. Sometimes the API returns timeout errors. You want Claude to handle this gracefully without stopping the conversation.
question: What is the recommended pattern for returning tool execution failures to Claude?
options: Return an error message to the user and ask them to retry the entire conversation
Return a tool_result with is_error: true and a descriptive error message, allowing Claude to adapt its approach
Catch the error silently and return an empty result so Claude doesn't worry about it
Throw an unhandled exception to terminate the agentic loop immediately
explanation: Returning is_error: true with a descriptive message lets Claude reason about the failure and decide next steps (retry, use alternative, or inform user gracefully). Asking users to retry breaks flow entirely. Silent empty results mislead Claude into thinking the tool returned nothing. Throwing exceptions prevents graceful degradation.

scenario: You need to expose a local SQLite database and a file system to Claude via MCP. You want to understand the protocol's capability model.
question: What are the three core MCP primitives that a server can expose to clients?
options: Functions, Variables, and Events
Tools (model-initiated actions), Resources (app-loaded read-only data), and Prompts (user-selected templates)
Endpoints, Schemas, and Hooks
Commands, Queries, and Subscriptions
explanation: MCP defines three primitives: Tools (callable functions the model decides to invoke), Resources (read-only data the application loads into context), and Prompts (reusable templates the user explicitly selects). The other options use terminology from other protocols — GraphQL (subscriptions), REST (endpoints), event systems — that don't apply to MCP's design.

scenario: You want to quickly prototype an MCP server in Python that exposes a 'get_weather' tool. You need the simplest possible implementation with minimal boilerplate.
question: Which framework provides the most concise way to create an MCP server with tool registration?
options: Write a raw JSON-RPC server from scratch with socket handling and protocol implementation
Use FastMCP which provides decorator-based tool registration like @mcp.tool() for rapid MCP server development
Use Flask with a custom MCP middleware adapter layer
Use the anthropic Python SDK's built-in MCP server class
explanation: FastMCP provides a high-level Python framework with decorator-based tool registration (@mcp.tool()), making MCP server creation as simple as writing a decorated function. Raw JSON-RPC requires implementing the full protocol manually. Flask isn't MCP-aware. The anthropic SDK is an API client library for calling Claude, not an MCP server framework.

scenario: Two tools, analyze_content and analyze_document, have nearly identical one-line descriptions, and Claude keeps misrouting between them.
question: What is the primary cause and best fix?
options: The model is broken; switch models
Ambiguous/overlapping descriptions cause misrouting; rewrite them to clearly differentiate purpose, inputs, outputs, and when to use each (renaming if needed)
Add both tools to a second agent
Set tool_choice to 'none'
explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary signal for selection; near-identical descriptions cause misrouting. The fix is to differentiate them clearly - purpose, inputs, outputs, and usage boundaries - renaming where helpful (e.g., extract_web_results). Switching models (A) or duplicating tools (C) doesn't fix ambiguity, and 'none' (D) disables tools.

scenario: You are writing a tool description to maximize reliable selection.
question: What should a high-quality tool description include?
options: Only the tool's name
Purpose, expected input formats, example queries, edge cases, and when to use it vs similar alternatives
Just the output schema
A marketing tagline
explanation: Effective descriptions specify purpose, input formats, example queries, edge cases, and how the tool differs from alternatives - giving the model what it needs to choose correctly. A bare name (A), output-only (C), or a tagline (D) leaves selection unreliable.

scenario: A generic analyze_document tool is used for extraction, summarization, and claim verification, and results are inconsistent.
question: Which refactor improves reliability?
options: Add more text to the single tool's description
Split it into purpose-specific tools (extract_data_points, summarize_content, verify_claim_against_source) with defined input/output contracts
Remove the tool entirely
Route all three uses through Grep
explanation: Splitting an overloaded generic tool into purpose-specific tools with clear contracts improves selection and result quality. More description text (A) doesn't resolve the overload, removing it (C) loses capability, and Grep (D) can't perform these semantic operations.

scenario: A well-written tool description exists, but a keyword-sensitive line in the system prompt keeps steering Claude to the wrong tool.
question: What should you review?
options: Only the tool description
The system prompt for keyword-sensitive instructions that create unintended tool associations overriding good descriptions
The model temperature
The user's phrasing only
explanation: System-prompt wording can create keyword associations that override even good tool descriptions, so review it for such triggers. Focusing only on the description (A), temperature (C), or user phrasing (D) misses the actual cause in the system prompt.

scenario: An MCP tool fails and returns a generic 'Operation failed' message, so the agent can't decide what to do next.
question: What is the recommended error-response design?
options: Keep the generic message; less detail is safer
Return structured metadata: errorCategory (transient/validation/business/permission), isRetryable boolean, and a human-readable description
Return an empty success
Throw an unstructured exception
explanation: Structured error metadata (errorCategory, isRetryable, human-readable description) lets the agent choose the right recovery. Generic messages (A) block informed decisions, empty success (C) hides failures, and unstructured exceptions (D) crash the loop.

scenario: Your MCP tool blocked an action due to a business-rule violation (over-limit refund).
question: How should the error be represented so the agent responds appropriately?
options: isRetryable: true with a generic message
A business-category error with retriable: false and a customer-friendly explanation so the agent communicates rather than retries
A transient error so the agent retries
No error; silently succeed
explanation: Business-rule violations are non-retryable; marking retriable: false with a clear explanation lets the agent communicate the outcome instead of pointlessly retrying. Marking it retryable/transient (A, C) causes wasted retries, and silent success (D) violates the rule.

scenario: The MCP isError flag is central to failure handling.
question: What is its role?
options: It formats successful output
It signals to the agent that a tool call failed, so failures are communicated back distinctly from successful results
It caches results
It sets tool_choice
explanation: The isError flag communicates tool failures back to the agent, distinguishing errors from successful results. It doesn't format success (A), cache (C), or configure tool_choice (D).

scenario: A subagent distinguishes between 'could not access the service' and 'query succeeded but returned no rows.'
question: Why does this distinction matter for error handling?
options: It doesn't; both should be retried
Access failures may warrant retry decisions, while valid empty results are successful and should not trigger retries
Both should be treated as fatal
Empty results should always raise exceptions
explanation: Access failures may need retries, but a valid empty result is a successful query with no matches and shouldn't be retried. Treating both as retryable (A) or fatal (C) is wrong, and raising exceptions on empty results (D) misrepresents success.

scenario: An agent has been given 18 tools and its tool selection has become unreliable.
question: What does this illustrate and how do you fix it?
options: More tools always improve capability; keep all 18
Too many tools degrade selection reliability; scope each agent to the 4-5 tools it actually needs
Reduce max_tokens
Set tool_choice to 'any'
explanation: Giving an agent too many tools (e.g., 18) increases decision complexity and degrades selection; scoping to the ~4-5 relevant tools restores reliability. More tools aren't always better (A), and max_tokens (C) or tool_choice (D) don't address selection overload.

scenario: A synthesis agent that shouldn't search the web has been given web-search tools and keeps misusing them.
question: What principle guides the fix?
options: Give every agent every tool for flexibility
Scope tool access to each agent's role; remove tools outside its specialization to prevent misuse
Increase temperature so it self-corrects
Add a second synthesis agent
explanation: Agents with tools outside their specialization tend to misuse them; scoping tools to role prevents this. Universal tool access (A) causes exactly this problem, temperature (C) doesn't fix scope, and adding agents (D) doesn't remove the misused tools.

scenario: A generic fetch_url tool is being used to load documents and sometimes fetches invalid or unintended URLs.
question: Which replacement improves safety and reliability?
options: Keep fetch_url but add a prompt warning
Replace it with a constrained load_document tool that validates document URLs
Remove URL loading entirely
Give the agent Bash to curl URLs
explanation: Replacing a broad tool with a constrained alternative (load_document that validates URLs) reduces misuse while preserving the needed capability. A prompt warning (A) is probabilistic, removing loading (C) breaks the feature, and Bash/curl (D) widens the attack surface.

scenario: You want to guarantee the model calls a tool rather than replying with conversational text, though it may choose which tool.
question: Which tool_choice setting applies?
options: tool_choice: 'auto'
tool_choice: 'any'
tool_choice: 'none'
tool_choice: {"type":"tool","name":"x"}
explanation: tool_choice: 'any' forces a tool call while leaving the choice to the model. 'auto' (A) permits text, 'none' (C) forbids tools, and forcing a named tool (D) removes choice.

scenario: You want a shared MCP server available to the whole team with credentials injected without committing secrets.
question: How do you configure it?
options: Hard-code the token in .mcp.json and commit it
Configure the server in project-scoped .mcp.json using environment variable expansion (e.g., ${GITHUB_TOKEN}) for the credential
Put it in ~/.claude.json so only you have it
Store the token in CLAUDE.md
explanation: Project-scoped .mcp.json with env-var expansion (${GITHUB_TOKEN}) shares the server team-wide while keeping secrets out of version control. Hard-coding secrets (A) leaks them, user scope (C) doesn't share with the team, and CLAUDE.md (D) is not for credentials.

scenario: You are experimenting with a personal MCP server you don't want to impose on teammates.
question: Where should it be configured?
options: Project-scoped .mcp.json committed to the repo
User-scoped ~/.claude.json for personal/experimental servers
In every teammate's shell profile
In the root CLAUDE.md
explanation: Personal/experimental MCP servers belong in user-scoped ~/.claude.json so they don't affect the team. Project .mcp.json (A) would share them, teammates' profiles (C) is intrusive, and CLAUDE.md (D) is not an MCP config location.

scenario: You configure two MCP servers simultaneously and wonder whether the agent can use tools from both.
question: What is true about tool discovery across servers?
options: Only the first server's tools are available
Tools from all configured MCP servers are discovered at connection time and available simultaneously
You must manually enable each tool per turn
Servers are mutually exclusive
explanation: All configured MCP servers have their tools discovered at connection time and available to the agent simultaneously. It isn't limited to the first server (A), doesn't require per-turn enabling (C), and servers aren't mutually exclusive (D).

scenario: Your agent keeps preferring built-in Grep over a more capable MCP tool that could answer directly.
question: How do you encourage correct selection of the MCP tool?
options: Delete the Grep tool
Enhance the MCP tool's description to clearly explain its capabilities and outputs so the agent prefers it when appropriate
Lower temperature
Force tool_choice to Grep
explanation: Improving the MCP tool's description to detail its capabilities/outputs helps the agent prefer it over built-ins like Grep when appropriate. Deleting Grep (A) removes a useful tool, temperature (C) doesn't change selection logic, and forcing Grep (D) is the opposite of the goal.

scenario: You need a standard integration with Jira and are deciding between building a custom MCP server or using an existing community one.
question: What is the recommended approach?
options: Always build custom servers for full control
Prefer existing community MCP servers for standard integrations; reserve custom servers for team-specific workflows
Never use MCP for third-party tools
Use Bash to call Jira's API directly
explanation: For standard integrations like Jira, prefer existing community MCP servers and reserve custom builds for genuinely team-specific needs. Always-custom (A) wastes effort, avoiding MCP (C) forgoes its benefits, and raw Bash API calls (D) bypass structured tooling.

scenario: Your agents make many exploratory tool calls just to discover what data exists (issue lists, doc hierarchies, schemas).
question: Which MCP capability reduces these exploratory calls?
options: More tools
MCP resources that expose content catalogs so agents can see available data without exploratory tool calls
Higher max_tokens
tool_choice: 'any'
explanation: MCP resources expose content catalogs (issue summaries, doc hierarchies, schemas), giving agents visibility into available data and reducing exploratory tool calls. Adding tools (A) or tokens (C) doesn't provide a catalog, and tool_choice (D) doesn't expose data.

scenario: You are deciding whether to model a capability as an MCP tool or an MCP resource.
question: Which mapping is correct?
options: Use tools for content catalogs and resources for actions
Use resources for content catalogs (browsable data) and tools for actions (operations the agent performs)
Tools and resources are interchangeable
Always use tools; resources are deprecated
explanation: Resources expose browsable content catalogs, while tools perform actions/operations. Option A inverts this. They aren't interchangeable (C), and resources are a first-class MCP concept, not deprecated (D).

scenario: You need to search file contents across a codebase for all callers of a function.
question: Which built-in tool is appropriate?
options: Glob
Grep
Write
Edit
explanation: Grep searches file contents for patterns (like function names/callers), making it the right tool here. Glob (A) matches file paths by name/extension, and Write/Edit (C, D) modify files rather than search content.

scenario: You need to find all files matching a naming pattern like **/*.test.tsx.
question: Which built-in tool is appropriate?
options: Grep
Glob
Bash cat
Read
explanation: Glob matches file paths by name/extension patterns (**/*.test.tsx). Grep (A) searches contents, cat (C) prints a file, and Read (D) loads a specific file's contents - none of which enumerate files by pattern.

scenario: You try to modify a file with Edit, but the anchor text appears multiple times and Edit can't uniquely match it.
question: What is the recommended fallback?
options: Give up on the change
Use Read to load the full file, then Write the updated contents
Use Grep to overwrite the file
Use Glob to modify the file
explanation: When Edit can't find a unique anchor, Read the full file then Write the modified contents as a reliable fallback. Giving up (A) is unnecessary, and Grep (C) / Glob (D) don't modify file contents.

scenario: You are exploring a large unfamiliar codebase and want to build understanding without reading everything upfront.
question: Which incremental strategy is recommended?
options: Read every file first, then reason
Start with Grep to find entry points, then Read to follow imports and trace flows incrementally
Glob all files and load them into context
Use Bash to print the whole repo
explanation: Incremental understanding - Grep for entry points, then Read to follow imports and trace flows - avoids context exhaustion and builds a targeted mental model. Reading everything (A), loading all files (C), or dumping the repo (D) floods context and wastes budget.

scenario: You must trace how a function is used across wrapper modules that re-export it under different names.
question: Which approach works?
options: Read one file and assume it's the only usage
First identify all exported names, then Grep for each name across the codebase to trace usage
Glob for the function's file only
Rely on the model's memory of the code
explanation: Tracing usage through wrappers requires identifying all exported names, then Grepping each across the codebase. Reading one file (A) misses re-exports, Glob for one file (C) doesn't trace usage, and relying on memory (D) is unreliable for specific code.

scenario: A synthesis agent occasionally needs simple fact verification, but full web tools would over-provision it.
question: How do you balance capability and least privilege?
options: Give it all web tools
Provide a scoped verify_fact tool for high-frequency simple lookups while routing complex verifications through the coordinator
Give it no verification ability
Set tool_choice: 'none'
explanation: A scoped cross-role tool (verify_fact) covers the common simple case without over-provisioning, while complex cases route through the coordinator. Full web tools (A) violate least privilege, no verification (C) breaks the need, and 'none' (D) disables tools.

scenario: You want to force extract_metadata to run before enrichment tools within a tool-using agent.
question: Which tool_choice configuration guarantees the first call?
options: tool_choice: 'auto'
tool_choice: {"type":"tool","name":"extract_metadata"}
tool_choice: 'any'
tool_choice: 'none'
explanation: Forcing the named tool (extract_metadata) guarantees it runs first; later steps proceed in follow-up turns. 'auto' (A) may return text, 'any' (C) may call a different tool, and 'none' (D) forbids tools.

scenario: An MCP tool experiences a timeout talking to a backend service.
question: Which errorCategory best fits, and what should isRetryable be?
options: validation; isRetryable false
transient; isRetryable true
business; isRetryable false
permission; isRetryable false
explanation: A backend timeout is a transient error and is typically retryable, so category 'transient' with isRetryable true is appropriate. Validation (A) and business (C) errors are input/policy issues (non-retryable), and permission (D) requires access changes, not a retry.

scenario: Your team gives a coordinator many cross-role tools 'just in case,' and selection quality drops.
question: Which distribution strategy is better?
options: Maximize tools on the coordinator
Scope tools per role with only limited cross-role tools for specific high-frequency needs
Put all tools on every subagent
Remove all tools from subagents
explanation: Scoping tools per role, with only a few cross-role tools for genuine high-frequency needs, preserves selection quality. Maximizing tools (A) or putting all tools everywhere (C) degrades selection, and removing all subagent tools (D) breaks their function.

scenario: You must decide when to consolidate two tools into one versus keep them separate.
question: Which guidance is soundest?
options: Always consolidate to reduce count
Split when responsibilities/contracts differ enough to cause ambiguity; consolidate only when they truly share one clear purpose
Always split every tool as finely as possible
Tool boundaries don't affect selection
explanation: Boundaries should follow clear, distinct contracts: split when consolidation causes ambiguity, consolidate only when there's a single clear purpose. Always-consolidate (A) reintroduces overload, always-split (C) creates needless fragmentation, and boundaries clearly affect selection (D).

scenario: A tool description omits input format details, so the agent frequently passes malformed inputs.
question: What is the fix?
options: Add retries around the tool
Include input formats, example queries, and edge cases in the tool description so the agent supplies correct inputs
Lower temperature
Force the tool with tool_choice
explanation: Documenting input formats, example queries, and edge cases in the description helps the agent supply correct inputs. Retries (A) don't teach correct inputs, temperature (C) doesn't fix format knowledge, and forcing the tool (D) doesn't ensure valid arguments.
